triage_reasoning: CD63 is a four-pass tetraspanin (TM4SF) that is predominantly resident in late endosomes/lysosomes and intracellular vesicles under steady-state conditions, with well-documented surface exposure via lysosomal exocytosis (e.g., in platelets upon activation, in mast cells after degranulation, and in various cancer cell lines). Flow cytometry on non-permeabilized cells confirms a PM pool, but the dominant compartment is lysosomal/endosomal. This fits the 'lysosomal_exocytosis' contextual bucket: lysosomal exocytosis drives CD63 to the cell surface transiently upon cellular activation or secretion. There is also a constitutive partial PM pool detected by surface biotinylation and flow, so dual_localization is plausible, but the classical story is lysosomal exocytosis bringing it to the surface. Multiple antibody-based and diagnostic tools target cell-surface CD63. No stable GPI or exclusive PM localization; inner leaflet anchoring is not the mechanism. The 'yes' verdict is disfavored because the dominant steady-state localization is intracellular (lysosomal).

executive_summary.one_paragraph: CD63 is state-dependently surface-accessible as a pan-tissue tetraspanin whose dominant steady-state localization is lysosomal/endosomal but whose surface pool expands markedly upon cellular activation. Direct multi-method support: KO-validated live-cell flow cytometry on HEK293T cells (a1_evi_06, a1_evi_07), and primary human granulocytes — basophils (a1_evi_15, a1_evi_16) and eosinophils (a1_evi_19) — across multiple independent studies. Surface levels are highly state-modulated: absent-to-low on resting granulocytes and stromal cells, rising sharply after IgE-mediated degranulation, platelet activation, TCR stimulation, and in disease states including HES, NPC, and the CRC tumor microenvironment (a2_evi_01, a2_evi_02, a2_evi_09, a2_evi_16). Moderate epitope masking via glycan shielding of the EC2 loop and TEM partner occlusion is the principal binder-engineering caveat (a2_evi_30, a1_evi_04); shed and secreted forms are absent, ruling out soluble decoy concerns.
executive_summary.accessibility_context_summary: Surface-accessible on activated granulocytes (basophils, eosinophils, mast cells), platelets, T cells, and macrophages upon degranulation or antigen stimulation; predominantly intracellular (lysosomal/MVB) at rest across pan-tissue expression.

filters.expression_level_rationale: Pan-tissue transcript screen placed CD63 in the top 50 surface genes (a2_evi_24); live-cell flow shows moderate surface signal on HEK293T and primary granulocytes (a1_evi_06, a1_evi_19), with dominant intracellular pool confirming moderate rather than high surface-level expression at steady state.
filters.expression_breadth_rationale: Transcriptomic screen excluded CD63 specifically for non-tissue-specific ubiquitous expression (a2_evi_24); protein detected in granulocytes, platelets, T cells, macrophages, MSCs, astrocytes, corneal epithelium, and lung alveoli across multiple independent studies (a1_evi_06, a1_evi_15, a1_evi_17, a1_evi_19, a2_evi_11, a2_evi_25).
filters.surface_specificity_rationale: Dominant steady-state localization is lysosomal/MVB (a1_evi_23, a2_evi_27); surface pool is activation-gated via lysosomal exocytosis across all major cell types examined (a2_evi_01, a2_evi_02). A constitutive minor PM pool is present in HEK293T (a1_evi_06) but the overall distribution is predominantly intracellular.
filters.has_known_ligand_rationale: TIMP1 is a validated endogenous binding partner that engages CD63 (together with β1-integrin) on macrophage surfaces to activate AKT/mTOR signaling in the CRC pre-metastatic niche (a2_evi_16); CD63 also serves as an entry receptor for SARS-CoV-2 on T cells (a2_evi_17).
filters.low_endogenous_expression_rationale: Pan-tissue transcript screen placed CD63 in the top 50 surface genes (a2_evi_24); live-cell flow shows moderate surface signal on HEK293T and primary granulocytes (a1_evi_06, a1_evi_19), with dominant intracellular pool confirming moderate rather than high surface-level expression at steady state.
filters.overexpression_surface_localization_obse: No method observation pairs an overexpression/mixed expression system with a direct or supportive surface-accessibility readout.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: CD63 surface accessibility is supported by multiple direct method types across independent sources, warranting `direct_multi_method`. Method type 1 — live-cell flow cytometry: CD63 positivity on intact WT HEK293T cells with CRISPR PanKO negative controls confirming antibody specificity (a1_evi_06, a1_evi_07); surface CD63 on primary human eosinophils by whole-blood flow (a1_evi_19); surface CD63 on primary human basophils (a1_evi_15, a1_evi_16); surface CD63 on platelets (a1_evi_17); primary human eosinophils in severe asthma (a1_evi_14). Method type 2 — IHC of human corneal/limbal tissue (a1_evi_20, a1_evi_21), perm status unspecified but consistent with membranous localization. The dominant steady-state localization is intracellular (lysosomes/MVBs, a1_evi_23, a1_evi_30), confirming the state-conditional (activation/degranulation-driven) character of surface exposure (a1_evi_12, a1_evi_13). This is not a contradiction — it is state-dependent dual localization. Several EV-surface observations (a1_evi_09, a1_evi_25–27) are excluded as EV-particle display, not live-cell PM. Grade: `direct_multi_method`.

claim_stances.note: Canonical tetraspanin topology (4TM, EC1, EC2, intracellular termini) — baseline topology reference, not a surface-accessibility measurement.
ECD loop confirmed accessible for nanobody insertion in engineered construct; describes topology, not direct live-cell PM staining.
Glycopeptides from large ECD loop confirm extracellular topology; method targets EV-surface CD63, not live-cell PM.
TEM complex formation may occlude ECD epitopes — ambiguous contextual note, not a direct surface claim.
Live-cell APC-antibody flow cytometry on HEK293T cells; methodological basis for KO-validated surface detection.
Live-cell flow cytometry: CD63 surface positivity on WT HEK293T; KO-validated (see a1_evi_07).
CRISPR PanKO negative control confirms antibody specificity for surface CD63 on live HEK293T cells.
HeLa CD63-KO as negative control for surface assays; validates antibody specificity.
CD63 surface detection on EVs from MDA-MB-231/HeLa/A549 — EV surface, not live-cell PM; excluded as EV.
Antibody reagent list; permeabilization status and cell-surface vs. intracellular use unspecified.
Western blot antibody identifiers (MEM-259, NK1/C3); whole-cell protein detection, not surface evidence.
Live-cell flow cytometry on RBL SX-38 (non-human); activation-driven 3-fold increase in CD63+ cells upon degranulation.
Review assertion: CD63 translocates to cell surface upon degranulation in eosinophils (lysosomal exocytosis mechanism).
Live-cell flow cytometry on primary human circulating eosinophils (severe asthma); direct surface detection.
Live-cell flow cytometry on primary human basophils; CD63 surface expression measured at baseline and post-omalizumab.
Live-cell flow cytometry on primary human basophils; CD63 upregulation after fMLP/anti-FcεRI/mastoparan/compound 48/80.
Live-cell flow cytometry on platelets; DHM reduces surface CD63 alongside CD62P and CD40L.
Antibody reagent identifier for mouse platelet flow assay; methodological detail only.
Live-cell flow cytometry on primary human eosinophils (whole blood, HES patients); CD63 in multi-marker surface panel.
IHC of human corneal tissue; CD63 protein detection — permeabilization unspecified, supports surface/membrane localization.
IHC of human limbal tissue showing strong CD63 expression; perm status unspecified, consistent with membrane localization.
RNA-seq: Cd63 highly expressed in mouse Abcb5+ limbal stem cells; RNA-level, no surface assay.
Permeabilized IF on hUCMSCs: dominant CD63 pool in MVBs/lysosomes. Confirms intracellular-dominant localization; does not refute activation-state surface pool.
IF on MSCs (perm status unspecified); vesicular compartment pattern — intracellular pool visible, not a surface-accessibility claim.
SP-IRIS on bovine colostrum-derived EVs; EV surface — excluded as EV display, not live-cell PM.
FIDA of anti-CD63 binding to engineered HEK/ciMSC EVs; EV surface, not live-cell PM.
Quantitative CD63 EC50 on EV surfaces; EV surface display, not live-cell PM.
Western blot of CD63-NLuc overexpression in MCF10DCIS; bulk protein detection, glycosylation pattern confirmed.
CD63 identified as T-cell surface host factor for ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 entry; functional evidence for surface accessibility in T cells.
Lysosomal degradation of CD63 after ANXA2 loss (bafilomycin rescue); confirms dominant lysosomal pool, does not refute state-conditional surface pool.

antibodies.name: anti-CD63
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: APC-conjugated; epitope inferred extracellular from live-cell staining on intact cells. Validated by CRISPR/Cas9 triple tetraspanin PanKO HEK293T showing complete loss of signal.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T wild-type cells — CD63 positive on cell surface by live-cell flow cytometry
HEK293T PanKO cells (CRISPR/Cas9 triple KO for CD9, CD63, CD81) — no detectable CD63 surface staining, confirming antibody specificity
Alpha-gal knockout RBL SX-38 cells sensitized with AGS sera, stimulated with alpha-gal antigen — CD63+ cells increased ~3-fold vs. control serum-sensitized cells upon degranulation
Circulating eosinophils from severe asthma patients — CD63 surface expression assessed by flow cytometry alongside HLA-DR
Blood basophils from allergic rhinitis patients — FcεRI, CD63, and CD203c surface expression measured at baseline and after 12 weeks of omalizumab treatment
Human basophils — CD63 surface upregulation measured as %CD63+ cells after stimulation with fMLP, anti-FcεRI antibody, mastoparan, and compound 48/80 (degranulation marker)

antibodies.name: anti-mouse CD63
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: APC-conjugated; anti-mouse CD63 from BD Biosciences; clone not specified in ledger

expression_observations.context: Platelets — CD63 surface expression detected by flow cytometry; dihydromyricetin (DHM) significantly reduced surface CD63 alongside CD62P and CD40L
Whole blood eosinophils from hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) patients — CD63 surface expression quantified in a panel including CD69, CD62L, CD66b, CD107a/b, CCR3, CXCR4, CD101, CD274
Human corneal limbal epithelial cells — strong CD63 expression localized to the limbal region by immunostaining; permeabilization status unspecified
Human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs) — CD63 predominantly localized to multivesicular bodies (MVBs), the primary precursors of EVs; dominant intracellular pool

antibodies.name: anti-CD63
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Clone H5C6; recognizes extracellular epitope (large extracellular loop, aa 33-51 and 103-203). BV421-conjugated.

antibodies.name: anti-CD63
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Invitrogen datasheet confirms extracellular/luminal epitope. AF488-conjugated.

expression_observations.context: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) — CD63-positive compartments (green) with vesicular pattern; SMase-treated MSCs retained comparable CD63 expression levels; permeabilization status not specified

antibodies.name: anti-CD63
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Mouse monoclonal. Immunogen is a cell preparation containing CD63 protein (proprietary); exact epitope region not disclosed by vendor.

antibodies.name: anti-CD63
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Mouse monoclonal; Abcam-listed as KO-validated (ab1318). Immunogen is a cell preparation containing CD63 protein; exact epitope region not disclosed.

expression_observations.context: MCF10DCIS cells transduced with CD63-NLuc fusion under CMV promoter — WB showed characteristic broad glycosylated CD63 band (30-60 kDa); 2.01-fold increase over parental control

overexpression.signal_peptide_detail: native CD63 cDNA sequence (full-length CD63-NLuc construct, no foreign leader replacement mentioned)

expression_observations.context: Primary human T cells — CD63 identified as a conserved T-cell host factor supporting ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 entry, implying surface accessibility on intact T cells

Cancer cell lines — CD63 protein levels reduced by ANXA2 loss; restored by bafilomycin A1 (lysosomal acidification blockade) but not ubiquitination inhibition, demonstrating dominant lysosomal-dependent turnover

non_surface_expression.context: MCF10DCIS cancer cell line — Western blot of overexpressed CD63-NLuc

contradicting_evidence.claim: In human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs), CD63 is predominantly localized to multivesicular bodies (MVBs) intracellularly rather than at the cell surface. This dominant intracellular/endosomal pool suggests that surface CD63 in this cell type is a minor fraction, qualifying the surface pool as contextual rather than constitutive.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: CD63 is a well-established lysosomal/late-endosomal tetraspanin that cycles between intracellular compartments and the plasma membrane. The predominant MVB localization seen in hUCMSCs by permeabilized immunofluorescence likely reflects the large intracellular reservoir, while a smaller surface pool still exists but may require activation or specific stimuli to become the dominant localization.

contradicting_evidence.claim: In human cancer cell lines, a major pool of CD63 is actively routed through the lysosomal degradation pathway, as demonstrated by bafilomycin A1 rescuing CD63 protein levels after ANXA2 loss. This lysosome-dependent turnover is consistent with CD63 being predominantly intracellular/lysosomal rather than stably surface-resident.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: Lysosomal degradation of CD63 reflects its normal trafficking itinerary as a tetraspanin that shuttles between the plasma membrane and late endosomes/lysosomes. The degradation data indicate the size of the intracellular pool under specific regulatory conditions (ANXA2 loss) rather than a direct refutation of surface presence; constitutive surface CD63 is still well-documented across many cell types.

excluded_as_ligand_engagement.reason: CD63 detected on EV surfaces from MDA-MB-231/HeLa/A549 by single-EV flow — EV/exosome surface display, not live-cell plasma membrane.
CD63 detected on bovine colostrum-derived EV surfaces by SP-IRIS — extracellular vesicle display, not live-cell PM.
FIDA characterization of anti-CD63 binding to HEK293/ciMSC-derived EVs — EV surface antibody engagement, not live-cell PM.
CD63 EC50 quantification on engineered HEK/ciMSC EV surfaces — EV particle surface, not live-cell plasma membrane.

expression.cell_type: basal limbal epithelial cells (limbal stem cells)

accessibility_modulation.change: CD63 translocates from secretory granules (late endosomes/lysosomes) to the cell surface upon degranulation; serves as a reliable surrogate marker of eosinophil activation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface CD63 becomes accessible to extracellular binders (e.g. antibodies, flow cytometry) only after activation-triggered lysosomal exocytosis; resting eosinophils present minimal surface CD63.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: resting basophils / mast cells (CD63 stored in LROs/secretory granules)
accessibility_modulation.change: IgE-mediated activation induces surface expression of CD63 on basophils and mast cells via lysosomal-related organelle (LRO) exocytosis; measurable by flow cytometry on non-permeabilized cells.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD63 surface pool is activation-gated; extracellular binders gain access only after IgE-mediated degranulation. Baseline surface CD63 on resting cells is low/absent.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: basophils (RBL SX-38) sensitized with control serum
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: basophils sensitized with alpha-gal syndrome (AGS) sera + alpha-gal antigen stimulation
accessibility_modulation.change: Alpha-gal antigen stimulation of AGS-sera-sensitized basophils induces ~3-fold increase in surface CD63+ cells by flow cytometry, reflecting IgE-mediated lysosomal exocytosis/degranulation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Approximately 3-fold more cells display accessible surface CD63 after antigen challenge; AGS disease context amplifies the degranulation-dependent surface pool.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: resting blood basophils (moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis patients, baseline)
accessibility_modulation.change: Stimulant-induced surface CD63 on basophils is measured at baseline and 12 weeks after omalizumab (anti-IgE) treatment; anti-IgE therapy modulates the activation-induced surface CD63 response.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Anti-IgE therapy alters the magnitude of surface CD63 exposure after stimulation, showing the degranulation-driven surface pool is therapeutically modifiable.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: blood basophils from hypertryptasemia (HαT) or idiopathic systemic mastocytosis (ISM) patients
accessibility_modulation.change: CD63 surface upregulation in response to multiple stimuli (fMLP, anti-FcεRI, mastoparan, compound 48/80) differs between HαT, ISM, and healthy controls, revealing disease-state-dependent modulation of the degranulation surface CD63 response.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Disease state (HαT vs ISM) alters the magnitude of activation-induced surface CD63, meaning binder accessibility differs across these disease contexts.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: peripheral blood eosinophils from asymptomatic treated HES patients
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: peripheral blood eosinophils from symptomatic treated HES patients
accessibility_modulation.change: Surface CD63 on eosinophils shows heterogeneous patterns across HES clinical subtypes (MHES vs non-MHES), treatment status, and disease activity, reflecting variable degranulation-driven surface delivery.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface CD63 accessibility on eosinophils is modulated by HES disease activity; binders may encounter variable surface levels depending on clinical subtype and symptom status.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: resting platelets (type 2 diabetes mellitus mouse model)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: activated platelets in T2DM context (without DHM treatment)
accessibility_modulation.change: Platelet surface CD63 is elevated in a T2DM disease/activation context; dihydromyricetin (DHM) significantly reduces this surface expression, indicating that the surface CD63 pool in activated diabetic platelets is pharmacologically reducible.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In T2DM, platelet activation drives CD63 to the surface, expanding accessibility; pharmacological suppression of platelet activation reduces this surface pool.

accessibility_modulation.change: MCG-derived CD63+ exosomes are released at the immunological synapse only upon TCR-mediated stimulation; MVB-derived exosomes are released constitutively. TCR activation triggers surface/secretory delivery of CD63 at the synapse.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD63 appearance at the CTL-target contact surface is activation-gated by TCR signaling; extracellular binders would only detect synapse-localized CD63 after antigen-stimulated degranulation.

accessibility_modulation.change: In CTLs, CD63 localizes both to multivesicular bodies and to cytotoxic granules (secretory lysosomes), confirmed by co-localization with granzyme B by SIM microscopy. This dual intracellular distribution is CTL-specific.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In resting CTLs the surface CD63 pool is limited; upon activation, cytotoxic granule fusion delivers CD63 to the surface. This dual compartmentalization means surface accessibility is tightly coupled to degranulation state.

accessibility_modulation.change: In hUCMSCs, CD63 predominantly localizes to multivesicular bodies (MVBs), consistent with the canonical intracellular distribution in non-activated stromal cells where the lysosomal/endosomal compartment predominates over the plasma membrane.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: The predominantly intracellular (MVB) localization in non-activated mesenchymal stem cells means minimal surface CD63 is available to extracellular binders under basal conditions.

accessibility_modulation.change: The large extracellular loop of CD63 is heavily N-glycosylated; these glycans mask epitopes on the ECD and render them inaccessible to standard antibody-based or proteomic methods, as demonstrated by ENCOREP glycopeptide enrichment.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Heavy N-glycosylation of the CD63 ECD creates epitope masking that restricts antibody and proteomic access to the surface protein; binding strategies must account for glycan shielding.

accessibility_modulation.change: In Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) patient-derived fibroblasts and CSF, EVs are enriched for CD63 and LAMP1, indicating elevated lysosomal exocytosis/endolysosomal activity compared to healthy controls in this lysosomal storage disorder.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: NPC disease state drives increased CD63 surface exposure and EV shedding via enhanced lysosomal exocytosis; greater surface/EV-associated CD63 is available to extracellular binders in this disease context.

accessibility_modulation.change: CD63 is identified as a conserved T-cell host factor supporting ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 (Omicron) entry, implying surface CD63 on T lymphocytes is functionally present and exploited during viral infection.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface CD63 on T cells serves as a viral entry mediator in COVID-19/Omicron infection, indicating extracellular accessibility of the CD63 ECD on T lymphocytes in this infectious disease context.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: M2-polarized liver macrophages in colorectal cancer pre-metastatic niche
accessibility_modulation.change: In the CRC pre-metastatic niche, TIMP1 secreted by tumor cells engages surface CD63 (with β1-integrin) on liver macrophages to activate AKT/mTOR and stabilize M2 phenotype; CD63 surface engagement is functionally relevant in the tumor microenvironment.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface CD63 on liver macrophages is functionally accessible to tumor-secreted TIMP1 in the CRC microenvironment, supporting a role for CD63-targeted approaches in the tumor-associated macrophage context.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: alveolar tissue of unexposed control rats
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: alveolar tissue of rats chronically exposed to wood smoke (COPD model)
accessibility_modulation.change: Chronic wood smoke exposure markedly increases CD63 expression in rat alveoli and elevates exosome particle numbers in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, reflecting stress-induced lysosomal exocytosis in a COPD-relevant model.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Smoke-stress-induced lysosomal exocytosis increases surface/extracellular CD63 in the alveolar compartment; greater CD63 signal is detectable in lung tissue under this pathological stress.

accessibility_modulation.change: CD63 protein is predominantly present in basal epithelial cells of the human limbus (limbal stem cell niche) as shown by immunostaining, and Cd63 mRNA is elevated in quiescent Abcb5+ LSCs vs Abcb5- corneal cells by RNA-seq.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Within the corneal epithelium, CD63 surface/cellular presence is enriched in the limbal stem cell subpopulation; binders targeting CD63 in the ocular surface would preferentially engage the limbal basal layer.

biological_context.grade_rationale: All four A2 axes are well-populated from independent sources. Expression is mapped across ≥8 cell types/tissues (granulocytes, T cells, macrophages, LSCs, astrocytes, platelets, lung, cornea). Subcellular localization is pinned (late endosomes/lysosomes/MVBs basally; plasma membrane upon degranulation). Anatomical accessibility spans blood, CNS, ocular surface, lung, liver. Modulation evidence covers IgE activation, TCR stimulation, disease states (COPD, HES, AGS, NPC, stroke), and pharmacological interventions. Picture is internally coherent — activation-dependent surface delivery is the unifying mechanism across all cell types.

surface_bind.attribution: © Balbi et al., Correia lab — EPFL / Inria / Novo Nordisk

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: CD63 is a single-pass tetraspanin that traffics independently to the plasma membrane via lysosomal exocytosis (a1_evi_01, a2_evi_01). Flow cytometry on non-permeabilized WT HEK293T cells confirms surface expression without any co-receptor requirement (a1_evi_06). No obligate chaperone or escort protein for ER exit or surface trafficking is documented in the ledger.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: CD63 surface expression is documented by flow cytometry across multiple cell types including HEK293T, basophils, eosinophils, platelets, and T cells (a1_evi_06, a2_evi_08, a2_evi_09, a2_evi_10, a2_evi_17). A transcriptomic screen explicitly excluded CD63 due to its non-tissue-specific ubiquitous expression (a2_evi_24). No apical, junctional, or other restricted subdomain distribution is reported.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: ECD length 114 residues (60-199) -> moderate; computed deterministically from DeepTMHMM topology.

epitope_masking.rationale: CD63 assembles into tetraspanin-enriched microdomains (TEMs) with integrins, proteoglycans, and immunoglobulin superfamily members, which may partially occlude the EC2 loop within the complex (a1_evi_04). The large extracellular loop is highly N-glycosylated, and these glycans have been documented to render EC2 epitopes inaccessible to standard antibody and proteomic methods (a2_evi_30, a1_evi_03). The deterministic prior indicates no homo-oligomer (is_homo_oligomer=false), and the ledger contains no homodimerization evidence, so oligomerization is not included. TEM-partner masking is constitutive in expressing cells, raising severity to moderate.

evidence.claim: CD63 belongs to the tetraspanin superfamily characterized by four transmembrane domains (TM1-TM4), a small extracellular loop (EC1), a large extracellular loop (EC2), and short intracellular N- and C-termini. This is the canonical topology reference for tetraspanins including CD63.

spans.quote: Tetraspanins are a superfamily of transmembrane proteins, consisting of 33 members in humans, that are classically characterised by four transmembrane domains (TM1‐TM4), a small extracellular loop (EC1), a large extracellular loop (EC2), along with short intracellular N‐ and C‐termini [ 1 ].

evidence.claim: CD63 is described as an EV tetraspanin with 4 transmembrane domains; nanobody was inserted into the extracellular loop between the third and fourth TM domains, confirming the large extracellular loop is accessible on the outside of the membrane.

spans.quote: To establish a CD3 targeting EV platform for CAR transgene delivery, we developed a CD3ε Nb‐CD63 chimeric construct, which consists of the EV tetraspanin protein CD63, with a CD3ε Nb inserted into the extracellular loop between the third and fourth transmembrane domains (Figure 1A ).

evidence.claim: CD63 is described as highly glycosylated with a large extracellular loop. Glycopeptides derived from the large extracellular loop of CD63 were used as model targets accessible by the ENCOREP method, confirming that the large ECD loop is surface-exposed and accessible.

spans.quote: These glycopeptides, derived from the large extracellular loop of the highly glycosylated CD63, illustrate the type of targets that are otherwise inaccessible with current methods but can be readily obtained using ENCOREP.

evidence.claim: Tetraspanins including CD63 associate with various membrane proteins (integrins, proteoglycans, immunoglobulin superfamily members) to form tetraspanin-enriched microdomains (TEMs). This partner-protein co-clustering presents an epitope-masking risk where the ECD of CD63 may be partially occluded within a TEM complex.

spans.quote: A key characteristic of tetraspanins is their ability to associate with various membrane proteins, including integrins, proteoglycans and immunoglobulin superfamily members [ 3 , 4 ], to form molecular complexes known as tetraspanin‐enriched microdomains (TEMs) [ 5 ].

evidence.claim: APC-conjugated antibodies were used to stain cells and EVs by flow cytometry for tetraspanin (CD63) detection on intact HEK293T cells, establishing the surface-staining methodology.

spans.quote: To confirm the successful generation of single‐cell KO models for these tetraspanins, we stained both the cells and their corresponding EVs with APC‐conjugated antibodies.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometric analysis on live HEK293T wild-type cells showed CD63 positivity on the cell surface (alongside CD9 and CD81). This is direct surface-expression evidence for endogenous CD63 at the plasma membrane of intact cells.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: CRISPR/Cas9-generated triple tetraspanin knockout (PanKO) HEK293T cells showed complete absence of CD63 surface staining by flow cytometry, confirming antibody specificity. CD63-KO cells and their derived EVs were also negative for CD63. This KO-validation confirms the signal specificity for the surface-detection assay.

spans.quote: In contrast, PanKO cells (lacking the tetraspanins CD9, CD63, and CD81) showed no detectable staining for CD9, CD63, or CD81, confirming the knockout efficiency; this absence of tetraspanin expression was also observed in PanKO‐derived EVs, which lacked detectable levels of these markers (Figures 2B,C and S1D).

evidence.claim: HeLa CD63-KO cells generated by CRISPR/Cas9 (a single sub-clone from bulk population after 2 rounds of sorting) were used as a negative control for CD63 surface-detection assays, providing a genetic-KO validation strategy for CD63 antibody specificity.

spans.quote: HeLa cells KO for CD9 or CD63 generated by CRISPR/Cas9 method, and used as a pool of 5 clones obtained after two rounds of sorting (CD9, (Mathieu et al. 2021 )) or as one sub‐clone from the bulk population after 2 rounds of sorting (CD63 (Palmulli et al. 2024 )) have been previously described.

evidence.claim: Single-EV flow cytometry on EVs from MDA-MB-231, HeLa, and A549 cell lines detected variable proportions of CD63-bearing EVs using anti-CD63 antibodies. CD63 tetraspanin was surface-detected on EV membranes from multiple human cancer cell lines, confirming surface accessibility of the large extracellular loop.

spans.quote: We thus observed, depending on the cell source (human cell lines MDA-MB-231, HeLa, A549), variable proportions of EVs bearing none of the CD9, CD81 and CD63 tetraspanins often used to define EVs, and of single- and double-positive EVs for each of these markers.

evidence.claim: Anti-CD63 antibodies used in this study: CD63 Brilliant Violet 421 (BioLegend cat# 353029; RRID: AB_2687003) and CD63 Alexa Fluor 488 (Invitrogen cat# MA5-18149; RRID: AB_2539523). These clones are load-bearing antibody identifiers for flow cytometry surface-detection of CD63.

spans.quote: REAGENT or RESOURCE SOURCE IDENTIFIER Antibodies Mouse anti-V5-HRP Thermo Fisher Scientific Cat# 46-0708; RRID: AB_2556565 Mouse anti-beta-actin-HRP Thermo Fisher Scientific Cat# MA5-15739-HRP; RRID: AB_2537667 Mouse Anti-mCherry Abcam Cat# ab125096; RRID: AB_11133266 Mouse anti-FLAG-HRP Thermo Fisher Scientific Cat# MA1-91878-HRP; RRID: AB_2537626 Mouse anti-CD63 (MEM-259) Abcam Cat# ab8219; RRID: AB_306364 Mouse anti-CD63 (NK1/C3) Abcam Cat# ab1318; RRID: AB_2076626 Rabbit anti-ACE2 Thermo Fisher Scientific Cat# MA5-32307

evidence.claim: CD63 cell-surface expression was measured by flow cytometry on live intact cells (alpha-gal knockout RBL SX-38 cells sensitized with AGS sera). After alpha-gal antigen stimulation, the percentage of CD63+ cells increased 3-fold compared with control serum-sensitized cells, confirming CD63 surface upregulation upon degranulation/activation. This is direct flow cytometric surface evidence on live non-permeabilized cells.

spans.quote:
To assess effector cell activation, we measured cell surface expression of activation marker CD63 by flow cytometry and mediator release through β-hexosaminidase release assays.

Results

After alpha-gal antigen stimulation, the percentage of CD63+ alpha-gal knockout RBL SX-38 cells sensitized with AGS sera increased 3-fold compared with cells sensitized with control serum.

evidence.claim: CD63 translocates to the cell surface upon degranulation in eosinophils, making it a reliable surrogate marker for activation. This describes the lysosomal exocytosis mechanism driving CD63 to the plasma membrane as a transient but measurable surface pool.

spans.quote: Upon degranulation, CD63 translocates to the cell surface, making it a reliable surrogate marker for eosinophil activation in various allergic and inflammatory settings 3 .

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry was used to assess CD63 expression on the surface of circulating eosinophils in severe asthma patients. This is a direct surface-detection method on live primary cells from human blood.

spans.quote: Flow cytometry assessed CD63 and HLA-DR expression.

evidence.claim: CD63 surface expression on blood basophils was measured by flow cytometry at baseline and after 12 weeks of omalizumab treatment. This is direct flow cytometric surface evidence for CD63 on intact primary human basophils, measuring activation-driven surface translocation.

spans.quote:
FcεRI, CD63, and CD203c expression on blood basophils induced by stimulant were measured at baseline and at 12 weeks after omalizumab treatment using flow cytometry.

Results

The RQLQ score in OMA-AR/NB decreased from 33.0 ± 11.0 at baseline to 5.2 ± 4.2 at 12 weeks, while in OMA-AR/B, the score decreased from 33.8 ± 7.6 at baseline to 19.4 ± 5.9 at 12 weeks.

evidence.claim: Basophil activation was evaluated by CD63 upregulation measured as percentage CD63+ cells by flow cytometry on live intact basophils following stimulation with fMLP, anti-FcεRI antibody, mastoparan, and compound 48/80. This measures CD63 surface expression as a degranulation marker.

spans.quote:
Basophil activation was evaluated by CD63 upregulation following stimulation with N-formylmethionyl-leucyl-phenylalanine, anti-FcεRI antibody, mastoparan, and compound 48/80.

Results

Relative basophil proportions were higher in subjects with HαT than in subjects with ISM.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometric analysis confirmed CD63 surface expression on platelets; dihydromyricetin (DHM) significantly reduced platelet surface expression of CD63 (alongside CD62P and CD40L). This is direct flow cytometry surface evidence for CD63 on intact human platelets.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis revealed that DHM significantly reduced platelet surface expression of CD62P, CD63, and CD40 ligands, decreased integrin αIIbβ3 activation, and suppressed intraplatelet reactive oxygen species (ROS) production.

evidence.claim: APC-conjugated anti-mouse CD63 antibodies from BD Biosciences were used for flow cytometric surface detection of CD63 on platelets. This provides an antibody-reagent identifier for the platelet surface-detection assay.

spans.quote: APC-conjugated anti-mouse CD63 antibodies, FITC-conjugated anti-mouse CD62P antibodies and PE-conjugated anti-mouse CD40L antibodies were obtained from BD Biosciences (San Diego, CA).

evidence.claim: CD63 surface expression was quantified in whole blood by flow cytometry on live eosinophils as part of a panel of activation markers (CD69, CD62L, CD66b, CD107a, CD107b, CD63, CCR3, CXCR4, CD101, CD274). This is direct surface detection evidence on primary human eosinophils.

spans.quote:
Surface expression of various eosinophil markers, including CD69, CD62L, CD66b, CD107a, CD107b, CD63, CCR3, CXCR4, CD101, and CD274, were quantified in whole blood by flow cytometry.

Results

Absolute eosinophil counts were significantly elevated in symptomatic patients being treated for HES compared to asymptomatic patients being treated for HES.

evidence.claim: CD63 expression in human corneal tissue was assessed by immunostaining (IHC/IF). This is a tissue-based protein detection method for CD63.

spans.quote: CD63 expression in human corneal tissue was assessed by immunostaining.

evidence.claim: Immunostaining of human corneal tissue demonstrated strong CD63 expression localized to the limbal region. This is IHC-based protein-level detection of CD63 in a tissue compartment; permeabilization status unspecified so membrane vs. intracellular pool ambiguous.

spans.quote: Immunostaining of human corneal tissue demonstrated strong CD63 expression localized to the limbal region.

evidence.claim: RNA-seq analysis showed that Cd63 is highly expressed in mouse Abcb5-positive limbal stem cells (LSCs). This is RNA-level expression evidence without a surface fractionation step; it qualifies but does not confirm surface accessibility.

spans.quote: Cd63 is highly expressed in mouse Abcb5-positive limbal stem cells (LSCs).

evidence.claim: CD63 was found predominantly localized to multivesicular bodies (MVBs), the primary precursors of EVs, in human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells. This places the dominant CD63 pool intracellularly, consistent with the lysosomal/endosomal-dominant localization that qualifies the surface pool as contextual/activation-dependent.

spans.quote: Given the predominant localization of CD63 to MVBs, which are the primary precursors of EVs, these findings suggest that H89 may modulate EV secretion pathways in hUCMSCs.

evidence.claim: Immunofluorescence staining of CD63-positive compartments (green) in mesenchymal stem cells shows CD63 expression with a vesicular compartment pattern. Permeabilization status not specified; this represents total-cell IF without explicit membrane scoring, qualifying rather than confirming surface accessibility.

spans.quote: Immunofluorescence staining of CD63-positive compartments (green) and ganglioside GM1 (red) showed that SMase-treated MSCs retained comparable CD63 expression levels ( Figures 1 B and 1C) yet exhibited a significant increase in GM1 area ( Figure 1 D).

evidence.claim: Single-particle interferometric reflectance imaging sensing (SP-IRIS) revealed a population of 40-45 nm vesicles expressing both CD9 and CD63 in bovine colostrum-derived extracellular vesicles. This is direct detection of CD63 on the outer surface of small vesicles by an antibody-binding method.

spans.quote: Dynamic light scattering indicated an average BCEV diameter of 127.3 ± 9.5 nm, whereas single-particle interferometric reflectance imaging sensing revealed a smaller population of 40- to 45-nm vesicles expressing CD9 and CD63.

evidence.claim: FIDA (flow-induced dispersion analysis) was used to characterize anti-CD63 antibody binding to EVs from three sources: HEK cells expressing CD63-eGFP, HEK cells expressing CD63-NeonGreen-Fc domain, and ciMSC-derived EVs. This is a direct method for quantifying antibody-epitope binding on the outer surface of CD63-bearing vesicles.

spans.quote: Here, FIDA was applied to characterize EV-antibody interactions using anti-CD63 antibodies across three EV sources: EVs from genetically engineered HEK cells expressing CD63-eGFP or CD63-NeonGreen-Fc domain (CD63-NG-Fc), and EVs from clonally expanded immortalized mesenchymal stromal cells (ciMSCs).

evidence.claim: CD63-binding EC50 values were quantified on EV surfaces: 1.9×10^8 particles/mL for CD63-eGFP EVs and 8.4×10^8 to 2.4×10^9 particles/mL for ciMSC EVs. This demonstrates dose-dependent antibody engagement of CD63 epitopes on the outer EV membrane, providing quantitative surface accessibility evidence.

spans.quote: CD63-binding EC<sub>50 </sub>values were 1.9 × 10<sup>8</sup> particles/mL for CD63-eGFP EVs and 8.4 × 10<sup>8</sup> to 2.4 × 10<sup>9</sup> particles/mL for ciMSC EVs, indicating higher epitope abundance in engineered vesicles.

evidence.claim: Western blot of MCF10DCIS cells overexpressing CD63-NLuc fusion protein under CMV promoter showed the characteristic broad migration pattern of glycosylated CD63 (30-60 kDa), confirming post-translational glycosylation of the CD63 protein. Densitometric analysis confirmed 2.01-fold increase in total CD63 in transduced cells. OE construct used native CD63 cDNA sequence (endogenous-SP context).

spans.quote: As shown in Fig. 1 B, both parental MCF10DCIS and transduced MCF10DCIS CD63-NLuc (+CD63-NLuc) cells displayed the characteristic broad migration pattern associated with glycosylated CD63 (30–60 kDa), with densitometric analysis confirming a 2.01-fold increase in total CD63 abundance in + CD63-NLuc relative to the parental MCF10DCIS control.

evidence.claim: CD63 was identified as a conserved T-cell host factor supporting ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 entry in T cells. This frames CD63 as a surface receptor engaged by the virus on intact T-cell surfaces, providing indirect evidence for surface accessibility in T cells.

spans.quote:
Background

Severe Omicron cases present profound lymphocytopenia, suggesting variant-specific immune injury.

Results

We identify CD63 as a conserved T-cell host factor supporting ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 entry.

evidence.claim: CD63 undergoes lysosomal acidification-dependent degradation: bafilomycin A1 (lysosomal acidification blocker) restores CD63 protein levels after ANXA2 loss, while ubiquitination inhibition does not. This demonstrates that a major pool of CD63 is routed through the lysosomal pathway and actively degraded there, consistent with the dominant intracellular/lysosomal localization of CD63 rather than the cell surface.

spans.quote: Pharmacological blockade of lysosomal acidification with bafilomycin A1 restores CD63 levels and rescues exosomal PD-L1 output, whereas inhibition of ubiquitination with TAK-243 does not, suggesting that lysosome-dependent turnover is a major contributor to CD63 reduction after ANXA2 loss.

evidence.claim: CD63 is a tetraspanin stored in secretory granules (late endosomes/lysosomes) of eosinophils and translocates to the cell surface upon degranulation, making it a reliable surrogate marker for eosinophil activation in allergic and inflammatory settings. This is a canonical example of lysosomal exocytosis-driven surface delivery.

spans.quote: Upon degranulation, CD63 translocates to the cell surface, making it a reliable surrogate marker for eosinophil activation in various allergic and inflammatory settings 3 .

evidence.claim: CD63 is a tetraspanin enriched in late endosomes, lysosomes, and lysosomal-related organelles (LROs) including secretory granules in mast cells and basophils. Its surface expression is induced upon IgE-mediated activation, serving as an activation marker measured by flow cytometry on non-permeabilized cells.

spans.quote: Using an indirect basophil activation test (BAT), in which healthy donor basophils were stripped of native IgE and passively sensitized with plasma from volunteers with AGS, we showed that in an IgE-dependent fashion, alpha-gal-glycosylated compounds increased the percentage of human basophils expressing CD63 ( 5 ), a tetraspanin protein enriched in late endosomes, lysosomes, and lysosomal related organelles (LROs), including secretory granules in mast cells and basophils ( 6 , 7 ).

evidence.claim: In alpha-gal syndrome (AGS), IgE-mediated alpha-gal antigen stimulation of basophils induces a ~3-fold increase in surface CD63 expression (measured by flow cytometry on non-permeabilized cells) relative to control-serum-sensitized cells. CD63 upregulation reflects lysosomal exocytosis/degranulation upon activation.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: basophils (alpha-gal knockout RBL SX-38 cells sensitized with AGS sera)

spans.quote:
To assess effector cell activation, we measured cell surface expression of activation marker CD63 by flow cytometry and mediator release through β-hexosaminidase release assays.

Results

After alpha-gal antigen stimulation, the percentage of CD63+ alpha-gal knockout RBL SX-38 cells sensitized with AGS sera increased 3-fold compared with cells sensitized with control serum.

evidence.claim: Surface CD63 on circulating eosinophils is used as an activation marker in severe asthma patients; CD63 expression measured by flow cytometry may provide additional insight into eosinophil activation state and treatment response to biologic therapy.

spans.quote: Surface markers like CD63 and HLA-DR might provide additional insight into eosinophil activation and treatment response.

evidence.claim: CD63 surface expression on eosinophils has been linked to asthma disease severity and exacerbation risk, consistent with activation-dependent translocation from secretory granules to the plasma membrane. Its utility for tracking biologic treatment response in asthma is under investigation.

spans.quote: Its expression has been linked to disease severity and exacerbation risk in asthma, yet its utility in tracking treatment response remains underexplored 4 .

evidence.claim: During activation and degranulation, basophils express CD63 as a unique surface marker. This surface appearance is activation-induced (IgE-mediated signaling) and reflects lysosomal/granule exocytosis, characteristic of CD63 biology in granulocytes.

spans.quote: During activation and degranulation, basophils expressed unique surface markers such as CD63 and CD203c.

evidence.claim: In moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis patients, surface CD63 expression on blood basophils (induced by stimulant, measured by flow cytometry) was assessed at baseline and at 12 weeks after omalizumab treatment. This establishes CD63 as an activation-induced surface marker on basophils whose expression is modulated by anti-IgE therapy.

spans.quote:
FcεRI, CD63, and CD203c expression on blood basophils induced by stimulant were measured at baseline and at 12 weeks after omalizumab treatment using flow cytometry.

Results

The RQLQ score in OMA-AR/NB decreased from 33.0 ± 11.0 at baseline to 5.2 ± 4.2 at 12 weeks, while in OMA-AR/B, the score decreased from 33.8 ± 7.6 at baseline to 19.4 ± 5.9 at 12 weeks.

evidence.claim: In basophil activation testing, surface CD63 upregulation (flow cytometry on non-permeabilized cells) is used to quantify degranulation in response to multiple stimuli (fMLP, anti-FcεRI, mastoparan, compound 48/80). In hypertryptasemia (HαT) vs idiopathic systemic mastocytosis (ISM) vs healthy controls, differential CD63 responses reveal disease-state-dependent changes in surface accessibility.

spans.quote:
Basophil activation was evaluated by CD63 upregulation following stimulation with N-formylmethionyl-leucyl-phenylalanine, anti-FcεRI antibody, mastoparan, and compound 48/80.

Results

Relative basophil proportions were higher in subjects with HαT than in subjects with ISM.

evidence.claim: In hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) patients, surface CD63 expression on peripheral blood eosinophils is quantified by whole-blood flow cytometry as a degranulation marker. CD63 surface levels show heterogeneous patterns across clinical subtypes (MHES vs non-MHES), treatment status, and disease activity states.

spans.quote:
Surface expression of various eosinophil markers, including CD69, CD62L, CD66b, CD107a, CD107b, CD63, CCR3, CXCR4, CD101, and CD274, were quantified in whole blood by flow cytometry.

Results

Absolute eosinophil counts were significantly elevated in symptomatic patients being treated for HES compared to asymptomatic patients being treated for HES.

evidence.claim: Platelet surface CD63 expression is measurable by flow cytometry in a type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) disease context; dihydromyricetin (DHM) significantly reduces platelet surface CD63 expression, indicating that CD63 is present at the platelet surface in a disease-state-modulated fashion consistent with activation/secretion-dependent surface delivery.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis revealed that DHM significantly reduced platelet surface expression of CD62P, CD63, and CD40 ligands, decreased integrin αIIbβ3 activation, and suppressed intraplatelet reactive oxygen species (ROS) production.

evidence.claim: In human corneal tissue, CD63 protein is predominantly present in basal epithelial cells of the limbus (limbal stem cell niche), as demonstrated by immunostaining. This establishes CD63 as a marker of quiescent limbal stem cells (LSCs) in the human ocular surface epithelium.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: basal limbal epithelial cells (limbal stem cells)

spans.quote: The present study demonstrates that CD63 is predominantly present in basal epithelial cells in the human limbus.

evidence.claim: RNA-seq of flow cytometry-sorted mouse corneal epithelial cells shows that Cd63 mRNA is elevated in quiescent Abcb5-positive limbal stem cells (LSCs) compared to Abcb5-negative cells, confirming CD63 as a marker of the quiescent outer LSC population in the corneal epithelium.

spans.quote: Additionally, Abcb5-positive LSCs expressed elevated levels of Gpha2 , Ifitm3 , and Cd63 , which have been identified as additional markers of quiescent LSCs. 22 A recent study by Jiang et al. identified high Ifitm1 expression in LSCs and early transient amplifying (eTA) cells and described its role in promoting cell proliferation through the inhibition of Ovol1. 23 Consistent with these findings, our RNA-seq analysis revealed upregulation of Ifitm1 and downregulation of Ovol1 in Abcb5-positive cells (see Fig. 1 D).

evidence.claim: Knockdown of CD63 in cultured human limbal epithelial cells significantly reduces cell proliferation and decreases expression of corneal epithelium-enriched genes (KRT12, CLU, ALDH1A1, ALDH3A1, TGFBI, MYEOV), indicating that CD63 is functionally required for maintaining LSC proliferative capacity and epithelial identity.

spans.quote: Knockdown of CD63 in cultured human limbal epithelial cells resulted in reduced cell proliferation and significantly decreased expression of corneal epithelium-enriched genes, including KRT12, CLU, ALDH1A1, ALDH3A1, TGFBI, and MYEOV.

evidence.claim: In alveolar tissue of rats chronically exposed to wood smoke (WS), CD63 expression is markedly elevated and exosome particle numbers in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid are increased. This tissue-level CD63 upregulation reflects stress-induced lysosomal exocytosis in alveolar cells under a COPD-relevant exposure model.

spans.quote: Here, we show that chronic WS exposure in rats induces emphysematous lesions accompanied by markedly increased exosome release, reflected by elevated CD63 expression in alveoli and increased exosome particle number in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid.

evidence.claim: Co-localization of CD63 and ADAM10 is observed in alveolar regions of wood-smoke-exposed rats and in COPD patient lung tissue, indicating that CD63-positive compartments are present in human COPD lungs and that ADAM10 sheddase activity co-occurs with CD63 in these disease-state alveolar structures.

spans.quote: Co-localization of CD63 and ADAM10 was observed in alveolar regions of WS-exposed rats and COPD patient lungs.

evidence.claim: TIMP1 engages CD63 (alongside β1-integrin) on liver macrophages to activate AKT/mTOR signaling and stabilize the M2 (pro-tumorigenic) phenotype, establishing a hepatic pre-metastatic niche in colorectal cancer (CRC). CD63 surface expression on macrophages is functionally engaged by a tumor-secreted ligand in a disease context.

spans.quote:
Mechanistically, TIMP1 engaged CD63/β1-integrin on macrophages, activating AKT/mTOR signaling and stabilizing the M2 phenotype.

Conclusions

CRC-derived TIMP1 remodels liver macrophages via the CD63/β1-integrin-AKT/mTOR pathway to promote a hepatic pre-metastatic niche.

evidence.claim: CD63 is identified as a conserved T-cell host factor supporting ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 entry in Omicron infection. This implies CD63 is present at the surface of T lymphocytes in sufficient quantity to serve as a viral entry mediator, a cell-type context not commonly highlighted in steady-state CD63 biology.

spans.quote:
Background

Severe Omicron cases present profound lymphocytopenia, suggesting variant-specific immune injury.

Results

We identify CD63 as a conserved T-cell host factor supporting ACE2-independent SARS-CoV-2 entry.

evidence.claim: In cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs), CD63 (and CD81) tetraspanins are found not only on multivesicular bodies but also on cytotoxic granules (CGs), indicating that CD63 marks specialized secretory lysosomes in CTLs — a cell-type-specific compartmental distribution distinct from other cell types where CD63 is primarily an MVB/late endosome marker.

spans.quote: However, in cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs), tetraspanins exhibit reduced specificity and are also found on cytotoxic granules (CGs) (Metzelaar et al. 1991 ; Peters et al. 1991 ).

evidence.claim: Super-resolution structured illumination microscopy (SIM) of murine CTLs shows robust co-localization of CD63 with granzyme B (GzmB), confirming that CD63 localizes to cytotoxic granules (secretory lysosomes) in CTLs. CD81, by contrast, shows comparatively low co-localization with GzmB, indicating distinct compartmental distributions for these two tetraspanins.

spans.quote: SIM imaging revealed robust co‐localization of CD63 with GzmB, whereas the degree of co‐localization between CD81 and GzmB was comparatively low, as quantified by Manders’ overlap and the Pearson's correlation coefficient (Figure 1b ).

evidence.claim: CD63-marked exosomes from CTLs are released at the immunological synapse (IS) in a stimulus-dependent manner. Specifically, MCG-derived (multi-core granule-derived) CD63+ exosomes require TCR-mediated stimulation for release at the IS, whereas MVB-derived exosomes are released constitutively. This represents activation-induced surface/secretory delivery of CD63 at the CTL immunological synapse.

spans.quote: Through total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy, we demonstrated that, in contrast to MVB-derived exosomes, MCG-derived exosomes are released at the immunological synapse in a stimulus-dependent manner.

evidence.claim: In Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) patient-derived dermal fibroblasts and CSF, EVs are enriched for endolysosomal proteins CD63 and LAMP1, indicating elevated lysosomal exocytosis / endolysosomal activity in this lysosomal storage disorder. This disease-state upregulation of CD63 in EVs reflects increased exolysosomal trafficking compared to healthy controls.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: dermal fibroblasts and CSF (NPC patients vs healthy controls)

spans.quote: Direct stochastic imaging and multiplexed EV immunoassays revealed that NPC EVs were enriched for endolysosomal proteins (CD63, Lamp1).

evidence.claim: In hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) cells, ANXA2 loss reduces CD63 protein levels without a corresponding decrease in CD63 mRNA, and is accompanied by activation of lysosome/autophagy-associated programs. This indicates post-translational regulation of CD63 abundance via lysosomal turnover in a cancer cell context.

spans.quote: ANXA2 loss reduces CD63 protein levels without a corresponding decrease in CD63 mRNA and is accompanied by activation of lysosome/autophagy-associated programs.

evidence.claim: Pharmacological blockade of lysosomal acidification with bafilomycin A1 restores CD63 protein levels in ANXA2-depleted HCC cells, confirming that lysosome-dependent turnover is a major determinant of CD63 abundance. Inhibition of ubiquitination does not rescue CD63, distinguishing lysosomal from proteasomal degradation pathways for CD63.

spans.quote: Pharmacological blockade of lysosomal acidification with bafilomycin A1 restores CD63 levels and rescues exosomal PD-L1 output, whereas inhibition of ubiquitination with TAK-243 does not, suggesting that lysosome-dependent turnover is a major contributor to CD63 reduction after ANXA2 loss.

evidence.claim: A transcriptomic screen for tissue-specific cell surface molecules identified CD63 among the top 50 candidate genes but explicitly excluded it due to its non-tissue-specific (ubiquitous) expression pattern. This directly supports the conclusion that CD63 is broadly expressed across tissues rather than being tissue-restricted.

spans.quote: Among the top 50 genes, 8 encode cell surface molecules. 3 out of the 8 genes ( B2M , CD63 , and CD99 ) were excluded due to their non‐tissue specific expression pattern.

evidence.claim: A transgenic GFAP-CD63-GFP mouse model demonstrates astrocyte-specific expression and CD63 enrichment in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) within the brain and spinal cord. Following tamoxifen induction, GFP-tagged CD63 is robustly detected in these CNS compartments, establishing astrocytes as a major CD63-expressing cell type in the CNS.

spans.quote:
The mouse line was generated by crossing GFAP-CreERT2 mice with CD63-emGFPloxP/stop/loxP mice. This system enables Tamoxifen-inducible, astrocyte-specific expression of GFP-tagged CD63, a tetraspanin enriched in sEVs. The model allows visualization and quantification of astrocyte-derived CD63-positive sEVs in vivo.

Results

Following Tamoxifen induction, GFP expression was robustly detected in the brain and spinal cord.

evidence.claim: Ischemic stroke significantly increases astrocyte-derived CD63-positive sEVs in the ipsilesional cerebral hemisphere and stroke-impaired spinal cord, demonstrating disease-state-induced upregulation of CD63 EV release from astrocytes in the injured CNS. This is accompanied by enhanced neuronal endocytosis of astrocyte-derived sEVs.

spans.quote:
Immunogold transmission electron microscopy further identified GFP-positive sEVs within neurons, providing ultrastructural evidence of astrocyte-to-neuron vesicle transfer. As a proof-of-concept, ischemic stroke significantly increased astrocyte-derived sEVs in the ipsilesional cerebral hemisphere and the stroke-impaired side of the spinal cord, accompanied by enhanced neuronal endocytosis.

Comparison with existing methods

Current approaches rely primarily on in vitro EV isolation or nonspecific membrane dyes.

evidence.claim: In human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs), CD63 predominantly localizes to multivesicular bodies (MVBs), the primary precursors of exosomes. This subcellular localization is consistent with the canonical intracellular distribution of CD63 in non-activated stromal cells, where the lysosomal/endosomal compartment predominates over the plasma membrane.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs)

spans.quote: Given the predominant localization of CD63 to MVBs, which are the primary precursors of EVs, these findings suggest that H89 may modulate EV secretion pathways in hUCMSCs.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometric analysis of non-permeabilized HEK293T wild-type cells confirms that CD63 is present at the cell surface alongside CD9 and CD81. This provides direct evidence of a plasma membrane pool of CD63 in a proliferating cell line, consistent with the dual lysosomal/cell-surface localization described in the UniProt entry.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: Triple knockout of CD9, CD63, and CD81 (PanKO) in HEK293T cells abolishes surface staining for all three tetraspanins by flow cytometry, confirming that native surface CD63 detected in WT cells reflects genuine CD63 protein at the plasma membrane and not non-specific staining.

spans.quote: In contrast, PanKO cells (lacking the tetraspanins CD9, CD63, and CD81) showed no detectable staining for CD9, CD63, or CD81, confirming the knockout efficiency; this absence of tetraspanin expression was also observed in PanKO‐derived EVs, which lacked detectable levels of these markers (Figures 2B,C and S1D).

evidence.claim: CD63 has a large extracellular loop that is highly glycosylated; these N-glycans on the extracellular domain of CD63 can mask epitopes and render them otherwise inaccessible to standard antibody-based or proteomic methods. This glycan-masking is relevant to understanding the apparent surface accessibility of the CD63 ECD.

spans.quote: These glycopeptides, derived from the large extracellular loop of the highly glycosylated CD63, illustrate the type of targets that are otherwise inaccessible with current methods but can be readily obtained using ENCOREP.

evidence.claim: Mast cell secretory granules originate from the trans-Golgi region and undergo maturation by fusing with endosomes to form mature granules — the compartment in which CD63 (a tetraspanin enriched in late endosomes/LROs) resides prior to stimulus-induced surface delivery. This establishes the subcellular itinerary of CD63 in mast cells before degranulation.

spans.quote: Secretory granules originate from the trans‐Golgi region as immature granules and undergo maturation [ 4 ], during which they fuse with endosomes to form mature granules.

evidence.claim: In tear polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs) collected from participants with ocular allergy versus healthy controls, CD63 is used as a degranulation surface marker alongside CD66b, measured by flow cytometry. More tear leukocytes were collected after sleep in allergy patients, and CD63 surface expression is used to assess PMN activation state in the ocular surface compartment.

spans.quote:
Samples were stained with antibodies against degranulation markers (CD66b, CD63), adhesion markers (CD11b, CD54), eosinophil marker (CD193), and aging marker (CD184), and analysed by flow cytometry.

Results

Significantly more tear leukocytes were collected after a night of sleep from participants with ocular allergy compared to healthy participants (p = 0.024), while there was no difference in late afternoon collections.

standing: surface-reachability barriers (BBB / tumor penetration / luminal / accessibility)
standing: co-receptor / partner-dependency for surface trafficking
standing: state-conditional surface biology (activation / stress / disease / EMT / senescence / differentiation) — feeds accessibility_modulation
standing: normal-tissue surface expression (six high-consequence tox organs)

top_cell_types.cell_type: fibroblast of connective tissue of glandular part of prostate
neuroendocrine cell of epithelium of lobar bronchus
mucus secreting cell of tracheobronchial tree submucosal gland
